item.title: Chocolates, suki, meth and phones among prison contraband seized
item.description: A package containing marijuana, methamphetamine, suki, mobile phones, chocolates and snacks was intercepted after being thrown into the Suva Corrections Centre in Walu Bay this morning. The Fiji Corrections Service (FCS) said alert officers on duty intercepted the illegal package at about 11am, when inmates were moving from their dormitories to the kitchen mess hall for lunch.
item.content:encoded:
A package containing marijuana, methamphetamine, suki, mobile phones, chocolates and snacks was intercepted after being thrown into the Suva Corrections Centre in Walu Bay this morning.

The Fiji Corrections Service (FCS) said alert officers on duty intercepted the illegal package at about 11am, when inmates were moving from their dormitories to the kitchen mess hall for lunch.

According to FCS, the suspects deliberately targeted the prison’s lunch routine in an apparent attempt to smuggle contraband into the facility.

Among the items seized were 17 rolls of marijuana, two sachets of methamphetamine crystals and 45 rolls of suki.

Officers also recovered one touch-screen mobile phone, one button mobile phone, one thumb-sized phone and a charging cable.

The package further contained two packets of chocolate, two packets of chewing gum, two packets of Sunrise Bongo bean snacks and a gas lighter.

The Fiji Corrections Service said its surveillance systems and CCTV cameras captured images of the suspects and recorded their vehicle registration details.

Acting Commissioner of Corrections Auta Moceisuva condemned the incident, saying it undermined efforts to rehabilitate inmates.

“This is a prime example of the lack of support from members of the public who continue to fuel this illegal activity. We have identified the culprits through our security systems and CCTV, recorded their car registration numbers, and are now working with the Police to bring them to justice,” he said.

The Fiji Corrections Service is working with the Fiji Police Force to locate and arrest those involved.

FCS reiterated its zero-tolerance policy on prison contraband and said it will continue strengthening surveillance and security measures to prevent illegal items from entering correctional facilities.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Mortuary holds Mister X after DNA tests fail
item.description: THE Lautoka Hospital mortuary is holding an unidentified middle aged iTaukei man nicknamed Mister X whose body has remained unclaimed for four months. Due to his remains being in an advanced stage of decomposition when authorities recovered him, forensic identification has been close to impossible.
item.content:encoded:
THE Lautoka Hospital mortuary is holding an unidentified middle aged iTaukei man nicknamed Mister X whose body has remained unclaimed for four months.

Due to his remains being in an advanced stage of decomposition when authorities recovered him, forensic identification has been close to impossible.

Tagimoucia Funeral Services director Shalvin Rohit Krishna said DNA tests on the deceased have failed to provide a positive identification.

“Currently the DNA tests have been done on him but there have been no results,” said Mr Krishna.

“For these cases, the first thing done is DNA tests are taken and matched with missing persons reports.

“So far for Mr X, there has been no positive results.”

Mr Krishna said other bodies that have remained unclaimed at the morgue included foetuses.

“We also have two foetuses, and two baby heads that were found in Field 4 in Naikabula.

“For these types of cases they are handled by police, and they are kept at the mortuary under their supervision.”

Mr Krishna said they have been working closely with authorities to carry out burials of unclaimed bodies.

His staff with a police escort cremated a 21-year-old man who had been at the morgue since October, 2024.

“It is quite difficult to do these sorts of things ourselves because there is a lot of legal processes to follow.

“If it was up to us, I would bury these unclaimed bodies if they have been here for too long.”

Minister for Social Protection Sashi Kiran said they were saddened by the reports of the unclaimed bodies.

“We are very sad to know that there are foetuses which means they have been babies dumped after delivery, but we do need to understand a little bit more about that such as finding out what happened to the mothers,” she said.

“I want to say that we are very sad to note that a country that calls itself a very communal and spiritual country is not able to take charge in this matter.

“Every person has a family, a village or a community and I plead with the community and the country that if somebody passes on and even though they have their differences it is important that they at least get a peaceful rest and it has to be done by families and communities.

“And if they are struggling to do it, they can reach out to us for help, but it is sad to hear that there are many unclaimed lives that have contributed in their own way to the nation.”

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Swimming pool in limbo
item.description: THE Lautoka City Council has been tasked to look for further funding to complete the unfinished multi-million-dollar swimming pool. This after the Ministry of Local Government’s request for an additional allocation under the 2026-2027 National Budget to complete the swimming pool was rejected earlier this year.
item.content:encoded:
THE Lautoka City Council has been tasked to look for further funding to complete the unfinished multi-million-dollar swimming pool.

This after the Ministry of Local Government’s request for an additional allocation under the 2026-2027 National Budget to complete the swimming pool was rejected earlier this year.

Local Government Minister Maciu Nalumisa said as a result the LCC special administrators’ council had been requested to look within the council’s finances.

“We requested for one but there was no allocation,” said Mr Nalumisa.

“But now we are exploring funding opportunities internally.

“We have asked the chair and the special administrators of the Lautoka City Council to explore from within on how they can fund the Lautoka swimming pool.”

When asked whether this long awaited project would be completed, Mr Nalumisa did not give a timeline but gave assurances the pool will be done.

“Our target is to complete it but there are a lot of technical aspects that need to be addressed as we pursue to complete the development.”

Lautoka Residents and Ratepayers Association president Narayan Reddy said the question of whether the project would be completed has been on concerned taxpayer and rate payers’ minds.

“It is the million-dollar question at this point,” said Mr Reddy.

“The fact that now the council will have to look for its own funding for the project is quite concerning especially when there were proposals from private investors who were willing to step up and finish the job.

“But there was no response from the council and government.

“This was a government project to begin with and now its being given to the council to complete without any confirmation of where the funding will come from.”

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: 135 orders issued,47 enforced – report
item.description: A total of 135 removal orders were issued to immigration overstayers in Fiji between 2019 and 2023, but only 47 were enforced, according to figures tabled in Parliament last Friday. The data, provided by the Department of Immigration to the Standing Committee on Foreign Affairs and Defence, highlights an ongoing gap between removal orders issued and those carried out.
item.content:encoded:
A total of 135 removal orders were issued to immigration overstayers in Fiji between 2019 and 2023, but only 47 were enforced, according to figures tabled in Parliament last Friday.

The data, provided by the Department of Immigration to the Standing Committee on Foreign Affairs and Defence, highlights an ongoing gap between removal orders issued and those carried out.

The report shows 50 removal orders were issued in 2019-2020, before dropping to 14 in 2020-2021. The number then increased to 33 in 2021-2022 and 38 in 2022-2023, bringing the four-year total to 135.

Enforcement figures were significantly lower throughout the reporting period. Authorities enforced 24 removal orders in 2019-2020, followed by just two in 2020-2021. Enforcement improved to 21 cases over the final two reporting years, contributing to a total of 47 enforced removals.

According to the report, removal orders fluctuated over the four years, reaching their lowest point during the COVID-19 period before steadily increasing again.

The Department noted that enforcement activity also improved in the later years but continued to lag well behind the number of removal orders issued.

The report concludes that while Fiji has increased its efforts to address immigration overstayers, a significant gap remains between identifying individuals for removal and successfully enforcing those orders.

Revenue generated by Fiji’s Department of Immigration almost doubled over four years, reaching a record $23.38million in the 2022-2023 financial year as demand for passports, permits and visas surged following the reopening of international borders.

The figures were provided to the Standing Committee on Foreign Affairs and Defence and tabled in Parliament last Friday.

According to the Department, total revenue stood at $12.36million in the 2019-2020 financial year before falling sharply to $7.17million in 2020-2021, reflecting the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on international travel and immigration services.

As border restrictions eased, revenue rebounded strongly to $15.13million in 2021-2022 before climbing to a record $23.38million in 2022-2023.

The report attributes the recovery to increased demand for passports, permits and visas as international travel resumed and labour mobility opportunities expanded.

Passport services remained the Department’s largest source of revenue throughout the reporting period and recorded the strongest growth after the pandemic.

Permit revenue also remained a significant contributor, recovering steadily as businesses resumed recruiting overseas workers and economic activity strengthened.

Revenue from visa applications, citizenship services and search fees accounted for a smaller but consistent share of the Department’s earnings across the four financial years.

The Department said the figures demonstrate a clear pattern of disruption during the pandemic, followed by a rapid recovery driven by increased mobility and higher demand for immigration services.

It also credits improved service delivery and processing capacity for supporting the sharp increase in revenue while maintaining operational efficiency.

Passport applications triple after COVID

Passport applications in Fiji more than tripled following the reopening of international borders after the COVID-19 pandemic, with the Department of Immigration receiving almost 134,000 applications over 2022 and 2023 alone.

Figures provided by the Department to the Standing Committee on Foreign Affairs and Defence and tabled in Parliament last Friday show passport demand remained relatively stable between 2019 and 2021 before surging as international travel resumed.

The Department received 20,737 passport applications in 2019, 18,573 in 2020 and 18,852 in 2021.

Applications jumped to 64,841 in 2022 and rose further to 68,946 in 2023.

Passport issuance closely matched demand throughout the reporting period, with 19,459 passports issued in 2019, 18,846 in 2020, 18,546 in 2021, 63,720 in 2022 and 67,752 in 2023.

According to the report, the sharp increase in applications followed the reopening of Fiji’s borders after the pandemic and was further driven by growing participation in overseas labour mobility schemes.

The Department said the increased demand reflected more Fijians travelling overseas for employment and other opportunities.

Despite the sharp rise in applications, the report states the Department maintained high processing capacity and continued to issue passports at a rate closely matching demand.

The Department also clarified that it does not routinely deny passport applications.

Instead, applicants who do not meet the eligibility requirements such as individuals who have acquired foreign citizenship are guided through the appropriate citizenship processes.

Under the Passport Act, passports are issued only to bona fide Fiji citizens who meet the legal requirements.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Mareta to workon self-confidence
item.description: KOCOMA, Qamea, native Mareta Vukiwai Makulau is representing KuiViti at the Bula Festival 2026, using the platform to advocate for youth leadership in Fiji’s vital tourism sector. Born and raised in Qeleni, Taveuni, the 21-year-old is pursuing a Bachelor of Nursing Science degree at the University of Fiji.
item.content:encoded:
KOCOMA, Qamea, native Mareta Vukiwai Makulau is representing KuiViti at the Bula Festival 2026, using the platform to advocate for youth leadership in Fiji’s vital tourism sector.

Born and raised in Qeleni, Taveuni, the 21-year-old is pursuing a Bachelor of Nursing Science degree at the University of Fiji.

She is sponsored by KuiViti, a local contemporary fashion label, for the week-long festival which officially opened on Saturday.

Ms Makulau said she decided to join the Bula Festival to build her self-confidence, represent her community, and inspire other young women.

As a first-time contestant, she is hoping for a strong showing of community support at the festival events.

“I’m looking for them to come in numbers for the events, so they can come and support me since this is my first time being a contestant for the Bula Festival,” she said.

With her advocacy centred on empowering the younger generation to lead Fiji’s tourism industry, Ms Makulau is urging other young women to pursue their goals fearlessly.

“Just be yourself, step out of your comfort zone, never stop chasing your dreams.”

Ms Makulau will also be wearing special KuiViti pieces during the festival.

The festival was officially opened last Saturday at Prince Charles Park with a commemorative lighting of the Bula Festival torch.

Chief guest Fiji Airways chief strategy, transformation and risk officer Athil Narayan said the festival had a rich history of generosity, compassion and service.

“Through (Bula Festival’s) fundraising efforts, countless lives had been touched through support for health care, education, community development and those most in need,” he said.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: inDepth I Barriers to inclusion – Study exposes gaps in support for children with disabilities
item.description: DESPITE Fiji’s commitments under international human rights conventions, children with disabilities continue to face significant barriers in accessing education, healthcare and other essential support systems. This was highlighted during the presentation of the Baseline Study on the Rights of Children with Disabilities by Human Rights and Anti-Discrimination Commission training officer Irene Yuen.
item.content:encoded:
DESPITE Fiji’s commitments under international human rights conventions, children with disabilities continue to face significant barriers in accessing education, healthcare and other essential support systems.

This was highlighted during the presentation of the Baseline Study on the Rights of Children with Disabilities by Human Rights and Anti-Discrimination Commission training officer Irene Yuen.

Ms Yuen said the study showed there were gaps that needed urgent attention to ensure children were able to enjoy their rights equally.

The study focused on children under the age of 18, in line with the Convention on the Rights of the Child, and examined Fiji’s obligations under three major international conventions.

The conventions were on the rights of the child, the elimination of all forms of discrimination against women and the rights of persons with disabilities.

“These three international conventions, which the Government of Fiji has ratified, provided the guiding framework for this study,” Ms Yuen said.

The study reviewed Fiji’s progress and compliance with these commitments, including the Convention on the Rights of the Child, which Fiji ratified in 1993.

The research covered the Western, Northern and Central divisions, using a human rights-based approach guided by Gender Equality, Disability and Social Inclusion (GEDSI) principles.

She said a combination of methods was used to conduct the study.

“This includes consultations with government agencies, interviews with non-government organisations working with children with disabilities and service providers,” she said.

“There were also community consultations conducted, surveys, focus group discussions, case studies, desktop reviews and written submissions.”

The study was supported by organisations working directly with children with disabilities, including the Frank Hilton Organisation and the Fiji Disabled People’s Federation.

“These two organisations assisted us in many ways, particularly in reaching the children and families who contributed to this study and providing the data that formed the basis of this report.”

An advisory committee was also established to provide expertise, guidance and strategic direction throughout the study. The committee included representatives from disability organisations, development specialists and a young person with a disability.

However, Ms Yuen said the study faced several limitations, including challenges in reaching participants and obtaining responses from some stakeholders. Researchers reached out to 54 children with disabilities and their families, but 31 people from 30 families participated in interviews.

“Their decisions were respected because participation was voluntary.”

She said one key limitation was the under representation of girls with disabilities, meaning their specific experiences and challenges might not have been fully captured. Of the 16 government ministries and institutions contacted, seven did not respond, while two agreed but did not schedule consultations. The team also attempted to engage teachers’ unions and several special schools but did not receive responses. Eight faith-based organisations were contacted, but none provided input.

“The limited funding and tight timeframe meant that the study was conducted by a small team, restricting the scale of the data collection,” she said.

She said the study was not designed to determine the total number of children living with disabilities in Fiji.

Instead, it focused on capturing their voices, experiences and stories to provide a baseline for future research, policy development and improvements to services. The study reached out to 54 children with disabilities and their families, with researchers visiting 10 communities across Fiji.

Eight communities participated, including six villages and two settlements. The research team also consulted 34 organisations out of 57 contacted, including non-profit organisations, civil society organisations, special schools and organisations of persons with disabilities.

Government institutions and statutory bodies were also consulted. A total of 16 interviews were conducted across nine government ministries and institutions, while 16 town hall sessions were held across eight communities with 265 participants. The study also received 90 written submissions.

Ms Yuen said because there was no complete national database identifying where children with disabilities lived, the team worked with disability organisations to identify children and families.

Thirty children were interviewed, including eight girls and 22 boys.

The children had different disabilities, including cerebral palsy, Down syndrome, albinism, learning disabilities, intellectual disabilities, speech delays, hearing impairments, physical disabilities, global developmental delays and autism.

“The artwork displayed in the presentation was created by children with disabilities. Through their drawings, they shared their feelings and what they hope to do when they grow up.”

The study also interviewed 41 parents and caregivers, most of whom were mothers. Participants also included fathers, guardians, caregivers, two grandmothers, one grandfather and one aunt.

Community consultations were conducted to understand public attitudes and awareness about disability. Participants did not necessarily have to know about disability or have a child with a disability in their community, as the aim was to understand wider community perceptions.

In the Northern Division, researchers interviewed children and families in Labasa, visited families in Levuka, consulted two communities and engaged one organisation of persons with disabilities.

The study also received 90 online written submissions.

item.title: Substance under analysis
item.description: A PACKAGE containing a white powdery substance, reportedly marked with the letters “DHL” and recovered from Namena Island in Cakaudrove, has been sent to Suva for analysis. Police spokeswoman Ana Naisoro said the package, containing a white substance believed to be illicit drugs, was found washed ashore and recovered by police.
item.content:encoded:
A PACKAGE containing a white powdery substance, reportedly marked with the letters “DHL” and recovered from Namena Island in Cakaudrove, has been sent to Suva for analysis.

Police spokeswoman Ana Naisoro said the package, containing a white substance believed to be illicit drugs, was found washed ashore and recovered by police.

Ms Naisoro said officers from the Savusavu Police Station were deployed on Tuesday, July 7, after receiving reports of the discovery.

“The substance is yet to be tested. It is being brought to Suva,” she said.

The parcel was retrieved by crime scene investigation officers.

She thanked members of the public for promptly reporting suspicious packages.

She said 76 parcels recovered to date from the Lau Group, Qamea, Kadavu and Beqa had all tested positive for cocaine.

“The Fiji Police Force is liaising with the Australian Federal Police to ascertain the source and destination of the illicit commodities.”

Members of the public have been urged not to handle suspicious packages but to report them immediately by calling 1681.

Meanwhile, the Northern Division’s Operation Sasamaki team seized more than 5600 plants believed to be marijuana from a farm in the Qaravutu farming area.

A suspect has been taken into custody for questioning and investigations are continuing.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Carnival committee secures sponsor
item.description: THE Duavata Northern Crime Prevention Carnival has secured a three-year sponsorship commitment from Flour Mills of Fiji Ltd. This is a major boost to the annual event which aims to promote crime prevention and community awareness.
item.content:encoded:
THE Duavata Northern Crime Prevention Carnival has secured a three-year sponsorship commitment from Flour Mills of Fiji Ltd.

This is a major boost to the annual event which aims to promote crime prevention and community awareness.

Carnival committee president Satish Kumar announced the sponsorship during the launch of the carnival in Labasa.

Mr Kumar said the agreement followed numerous discussions.

“We will be signing the agreement soon. Last year FMF supported us in a smaller way, but this year they have come on board in a much bigger way for the FMF Duavata Northern Crime Prevention Carnival 2026,” he said.

The carnival is more than a fundraising event, he said, with contestants serving as ambassadors to educate communities on crime, particularly the growing threat of illicit drugs.

“The biggest threat we face today is drugs. These contestants will help educate people throughout the Northern Division. We proudly call ourselves the Friendly North, and we want to keep it that way.”

He said this year’s major community project would be the construction of a classroom at Valelawa in Dreketi where some students were still studying in tents.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.
